original_abstract: The selective modification of nitrogen-containing heteroaromatics is a cornerstone of chemical biology and drug discovery. While recent advancements have introduced strategies for skeletal expansion and contraction, methods that allow for the direct atom-for-atom exchange within these frameworks remain scarce. Here, we report a robust strategy for <sup>14</sup>N → <sup>15</sup>N isotopic exchange across a diverse range of aromatic nitrogen heterocycles. This "nitrogen transmutation" is achieved by activating the parent heterocycle via N-triflylation, followed by a ring-opening and ring-closing sequence mediated by <sup>15</sup>N-aspartate. A critical feature of this process is the formation of a stable, isolable <sup>15</sup>N-succinyl intermediate, which subsequently undergoes elimination to yield the isotopically labeled product. These reactions proceed under mild conditions, delivering high chemical yields and exceptional levels of isotopic enrichment.
humanized_abstract: Nitrogen-containing heteroaromatics are of great importance to the field of chemical biology and drug discovery. While advances have been made to achieve the selective modification through both elongation and shortening of the heteroaromatic ring, few methods have been reported that achieve direct atom-for-atom substitution of nitrogen atoms in these compounds. We now report the efficient isotropic exchange (14)N to (15)N of a wide range of aromatic nitrogen heterocycles via the "nitrogen transmutation" protocol, which relies on N-triflylation, a C–N ring-opening and ring-closing sequence catalyzed by (15)N-aspartate with subsequent formation of an isolable (15)N-succinyl derivative and the consequent (15)N-labeled heterocycle through a neutral elimination process. All reactions were performed at mild conditions (room temperature) with excellent isolated yields and high (15)N-enrichment levels of the final products.

All reactions were performed at mild conditions (room temperature) with excellent isolated yields and high (15)N-enrichment levels of the final products.
